title: नवग्रह स्तोत्र
devanagariText:
॥ श्री नवग्रह स्तोत्रम् ॥

॥ सूर्य ॥
जपाकुसुमसङ्काशं काश्यपेयं महाद्युतिम्।
तमोऽरिं सर्वपापघ्नं प्रणतोऽस्मि दिवाकरम्॥१॥

॥ चन्द्र ॥
दधिशङ्खतुषाराभं क्षीरोदार्णवसम्भवम्।
नमामि शशिनं सोमं शम्भोर्मुकुटभूषणम्॥२॥

॥ मंगल ॥
धरणीगर्भसम्भूतं विद्युत्कान्तिसमप्रभम्।
कुमारं शक्तिहस्तं तं मङ्गलं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥३॥

॥ बुध ॥
प्रियङ्गुकलिकाश्यामं रूपेणाप्रतिमं बुधम्।
सौम्यं सौम्यगुणोपेतं तं बुधं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥४॥

॥ बृहस्पति ॥
देवानां च ऋषीणां च गुरुं काञ्चनसन्निभम्।
बुद्धिभूतं त्रिलोकेशं तं नमामि बृहस्पतिम्॥५॥

॥ शुक्र ॥
हिमकुन्दमृणालाभं दैत्यानां परमं गुरुम्।
सर्वशास्त्रप्रवक्तारं भार्गवं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥६॥

॥ शनि ॥
नीलाञ्जनसमाभासं रविपुत्रं यमाग्रजम्।
छायामार्तण्डसम्भूतं तं नमामि शनैश्चरम्॥७॥

॥ राहु ॥
अर्धकायं महावीर्यं चन्द्रादित्यविमर्दनम्।
सिंहिकागर्भसम्भूतं तं राहुं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥८॥

॥ केतु ॥
पलाशपुष्पसङ्काशं तारकाग्रहमस्तकम्।
रौद्रं रौद्रात्मकं घोरं तं केतुं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥९॥

॥ फलश्रुति ॥
इति व्यासमुखोद्गीतं यः पठेत् सुसमाहितः।
दिवा वा यदि वा रात्रौ विघ्नशान्तिर्भविष्यति॥

नरनारीनृपाणां च भवेद्दुःस्वप्ननाशनम्।
ऐश्वर्यमतुलं तेषामारोग्यं पुष्टिवर्धनम्॥

ग्रहनक्षत्रजाः पीडास्तस्कराग्निसमुद्भवाः।
ताः सर्वाः प्रशमं यान्ति व्यासो ब्रूते न संशयः॥

॥ इति श्री व्यासविरचितं नवग्रह स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shrī Navagraha Stotram ||

|| Sūrya ||
Japākusumasaṅkāśaṁ Kāśyapeyaṁ mahādyutim.
Tamo'riṁ sarvapāpaghnaṁ praṇato'smi Divākaram.||1||

|| Chandra ||
Dadhiśaṅkhatuṣārābhaṁ kṣīrodārṇavasambhavam.
Namāmi Śaśinaṁ Somaṁ Śambhormukuṭabhūṣaṇam.||2||

|| Maṅgala ||
Dharaṇīgarbhasambhūtaṁ vidyutkāntisimaprabham.
Kumāraṁ śaktihastam taṁ Maṅgalaṁ praṇamāmyaham.||3||

|| Budha ||
Priyaṅgukalikāśyāmaṁ rūpeṇāpratimaṁ Budham.
Saumyaṁ saumyaguṇopetaṁ taṁ Budhaṁ praṇamāmyaham.||4||

|| Bṛhaspati ||
Devānāṁ cha ṛṣīṇāṁ cha guruṁ kāñchanasannibham.
Buddhibhūtaṁ trilokeśaṁ taṁ namāmi Bṛhaspatim.||5||

|| Śukra ||
Himakundamṛṇālābhaṁ daityānāṁ paramaṁ gurum.
Sarvaśāstrapravaktāraṁ Bhārgavaṁ praṇamāmyaham.||6||

|| Śani ||
Nīlāñjansamābhāsaṁ Raviputraṁ Yamāgrajam.
Chhāyāmārtaṇḍasambhūtaṁ taṁ namāmi Śanaiścharam.||7||

|| Rāhu ||
Ardhakāyaṁ mahāvīryaṁ Chandrādityavimardanam.
Simhikāgarbhasambhūtaṁ taṁ Rāhuṁ praṇamāmyaham.||8||

|| Ketu ||
Palāśapuṣpasaṅkāśaṁ tārakāgrahamastakam.
Raudraṁ raudrātmakaṁ ghoraṁ taṁ Ketuṁ praṇamāmyaham.||9||

|| Phalaśruti ||
Iti Vyāsamukhodgītaṁ yaḥ paṭhet susamāhitaḥ.
Divā vā yadi vā rātrau vighnaśāntirbhaviṣyati.

Naranārīnṛpāṇāṁ cha bhavedduhsvapnanāśanam.
Aiśvaryamatulaṁ teṣāmārogyaṁ puṣṭivardhanam.

Grahanakṣatrajāḥ pīḍāstaskarāgnisamudbhavāḥ.
Tāḥ sarvāḥ praśamaṁ yānti Vyāso brūte na saṁśayaḥ.

|| Iti Shrī Vyāsavirachitaṁ Navagraha Stotraṁ sampūrṇam ||
hindiMeaning: जपा (गुड़हल) के फूल के समान, कश्यप के पुत्र, महान तेजस्वी, अन्धकार के शत्रु, सब पापों का नाश करने वाले दिवाकर (सूर्य) को मैं प्रणाम करता हूँ। दही, शंख और तुषार के समान श्वेत, क्षीरसागर से उत्पन्न, शम्भु के मुकुट की भूषण शशि (चन्द्रमा) को मैं नमस्कार करता हूँ। धरती के गर्भ से उत्पन्न, विद्युत के समान कान्ति वाले, शक्ति हाथ में धारण करने वाले कुमार मंगल को मैं प्रणाम करता हूँ।
benefits: Traditionally recited for removal of afflictions caused by all nine planets
Traditionally recited for removal of bad dreams and nightmares
Invokes the blessing of good health and nourishment
Traditionally recited for removal of obstacles from thieves, fire, and planetary doshas
Composed by Sage Vyasa, guaranteed to bring peace
description: Dedicated to the Nine Planets (Navagraha), the Navagraha Stotra (नवग्रह स्तोत्र) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. This sacred composition is sourced from Vyasa - Skanda Purana and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to remove obstacles from thieves, fire, and planetary doshas as well as composed by Sage Vyasa, guaranteed to bring peace. Additionally, it is known to remove afflictions caused by all nine planets. With 9 verses and a reading time of about 5 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Navagraha Stotra in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Navagraha Stotra in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Nine planet prayer by Sage Vyasa for planetary remedy and removal of graha doshas.
alsoKnownAs: Nine Planets Stotra by Sage Vyasa

seoFaqs.question: What is the Navagraha Stotra and which planets does it cover?
seoFaqs.answer: The Navagraha Stotra is a nine-verse Sanskrit hymn composed by Sage Vyasa, with one verse dedicated to each of the nine planetary deities of Hindu astrology in the canonical order: Surya (Sun), Chandra (Moon), Mangal (Mars), Budha (Mercury), Brihaspati (Jupiter), Shukra (Venus), Shani (Saturn), Rahu (the ascending lunar node) and Ketu (the descending lunar node). Each verse describes the deity's iconographic colour, parentage, mount or symbol, and offers a salutation. The text closes with a three-verse phalashruti promising removal of obstacles, bad dreams, illness, and dangers from theft and fire. It is recited daily by Smarta households and is the standard text used in Navagraha-puja at temples. (Sources: Skanda Purana; Brahma Purana; vedadhara.com Vyasa Navagraha Stotra.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous opening verse Japakusumasankasham?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening verse — Japa-kusuma-sankasham Kashyapeyam Maha-dyutim, Tamo-arim sarva-papa-ghnam pranato'smi Divakaram — is one of the most universally recited Sun-prayer verses in all Hindu traditions. It salutes the Sun as resembling the bright red japa (hibiscus) flower, as the son of Sage Kashyapa, of great brilliance (Maha-dyuti), the enemy of darkness (Tamo-ari), the destroyer of all sins (sarva-papa-ghna). This single verse is the ritual opening of innumerable Surya-namaskara, Aditya-puja and Sandhya-vandanam practices across India. Many devotees who do not recite the full Navagraha Stotra still know and use this verse alone as their daily Sun-prayer at sunrise. (Sources: classical Surya-namaskara liturgy; Vyasa Navagraha Stotra verse 1.)

seoFaqs.question: How is this stotra different from the Navagraha Shanti Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Both texts come from the same Vyasa-virachita source and contain the identical nine planet verses. The differences are presentational: this Navagraha Stotra explicitly labels each verse with the planet's name as a heading (Surya, Chandra, Mangal etc.), making it easier for new reciters to identify which planet is being addressed; it also includes a more extensive three-verse phalashruti covering removal of duhsvapna (bad dreams), grahanakshatra-pida (planet-and-asterism-caused suffering), and dangers from taskara (thieves) and agni (fire). The Navagraha Shanti Stotram on the same site has only one closing phalashruti verse. Both are equally authoritative; the choice between them is editorial preference, with this version preferred when one wants the fuller phalashruti benefit-list. (Sources: comparative Vyasa Navagraha editions.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the phalashruti specifically promise?
seoFaqs.answer: The three-verse phalashruti is unusually rich. The first verse promises that recitation by day or night brings vighna-shanti (pacification of obstacles). The second adds three further benefits: duhsvapna-nashanam (destruction of bad dreams and nightmares — important in jyotisha because troubled sleep often signals graha affliction), aishvaryam-atulam (incomparable wealth and worldly prosperity), and arogyam pushti-vardhanam (good health and bodily nourishment). The third verse promises that all sufferings caused by planet-asterism combinations and by the dangers of thieves and fire are completely pacified — Vyasa himself declares this without doubt (Vyaso brute na samshayah). The closing va samshayah is a strong rhetorical guarantee found in only a few Puranic phalashrutis. (Sources: Navagraha Stotra phalashruti verses 1-3.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is each planet associated with a specific colour in the verses?
seoFaqs.answer: The colour assigned to each planet in the Vyasa stotra is canonical Vedic-jyotisha iconography reflecting the planet's nature: Surya is red-orange like hibiscus (rajas, fire, vitality), Chandra is white like curd-conch-snow (sattva, water, coolness), Mangal is lightning-bright crimson (rajas, fire, blood and warriorship), Budha is dark green like priyangu bud (mixed nature, intelligence), Brihaspati is golden (sattva, wisdom, prosperity), Shukra is white like jasmine (sattva, beauty, refinement), Shani is dark blue-black like nilanjana antimony (tamas, contraction, austerity), Rahu is shadow-dark (eclipsing power), and Ketu is fiery red like palasha flower (chaos, comet-like). These colours determine the gemstone, cloth, flower, food and metal offerings made to each planet in graha-shanti puja. (Sources: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra graha-svarupa; jyotisha iconography manuals.)

seoFaqs.question: Can different planets be worshipped on different weekdays using this stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes, this is the standard daily Navagraha sadhana in many Smarta households. The full nine-verse stotra is recited every day, but on each weekday extra emphasis (additional repetitions, mantra-japa, special offerings) is given to that day's planet: Sunday for Surya (verse 1), Monday for Chandra (verse 2), Tuesday for Mangal (verse 3), Wednesday for Budha (verse 4), Thursday for Brihaspati (verse 5), Friday for Shukra (verse 6), Saturday for Shani (verse 7). Rahu and Ketu (verses 8 and 9) do not have weekdays of their own but are honoured on Saturday (Shani-Rahu connection) and Tuesday (Mangal-Ketu connection), or on the Rahu-kala and Yama-kala periods of any day. This rotational practice keeps all nine grahas continuously pacified through the week. (Sources: Smarta nitya-karma paddhati; weekday vrat traditions.)

seoFaqs.question: Is Navagraha Stotra suitable for those without astrological knowledge?
seoFaqs.answer: Absolutely — and this is one of its greatest virtues. The Vyasa Navagraha Stotra was specifically composed as a comprehensive prayer that covers all nine planets equitably, so that even persons without any knowledge of their natal chart, dashas or doshas can recite it daily and receive the benefit of complete graha-shanti. It does not require any preliminary diagnosis by a jyotishi, no specific planetary identification, no expensive yajna or homa. A simple recitation after morning bath, ideally facing the rising sun or before a Navagraha murti or yantra, with a brief sankalpa for graha-shanti, is sufficient. The stotra's universality is precisely why it has become the most popular Navagraha prayer in modern Hindu households. (Sources: traditional Smarta-paddhati; popular bhakti commentaries.)
